male-16-torsion.name: James Donovan
male-16-torsion.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe pain in my left testicle that woke me up
male-16-torsion.variantDescription: Acute testicular torsion requiring emergent surgery

missedConsequences.Testicular Torsion: Testicular salvage rate drops dramatically after 6 hours. After 24 hours, nearly 0% salvage. This is a surgical emergency.
missedConsequences.Torsion of Appendix Testis: Benign but can mimic torsion. The difference is management: surgery vs. supportive care.
missedConsequences.Epididymitis in Adolescent: Less common in teens than adults. If misdiagnosed as epididymitis and it's actually torsion, testicle is lost.

commonPitfalls: Waiting for ultrasound if clinical suspicion is high - go to OR
Assuming epididymitis in an adolescent (torsion is more common in this age group)
Not recognizing intermittent torsion (pain that resolved spontaneously)
Forgetting that cremasteric reflex ABSENT = highly suggestive of torsion
Delaying urologic consultation

mcqs.question: A 16-year-old presents with 4 hours of severe left testicular pain. The left testicle is high-riding, horizontal lie, exquisitely tender with absent cremasteric reflex. What is the BEST next step?

options.explanation: If clinical picture is strongly suggestive of torsion, do NOT delay for ultrasound. Go directly to OR. Ultrasound takes time and a negative study doesn't rule out early/intermittent torsion.

options.text: Urology consult for emergent surgical exploration
options.explanation: This is the classic presentation of testicular torsion. Time is testicle! Go directly to OR. Each hour of delay decreases salvage rate. Clinical diagnosis is sufficient.

Epididymitis is less common in adolescents. This presentation (sudden onset, high-riding testicle, absent cremasteric reflex) is classic for torsion. Treating as epididymitis would result in testicular loss.
CT is not indicated for testicular pathology. Ultrasound is imaging of choice IF imaging is needed. But this patient should go to OR.

mcqs.question: What physical exam finding is MOST specific for testicular torsion?

options.explanation: Tenderness is present in both torsion and epididymitis. Not specific.
Absent cremasteric reflex is highly specific for torsion (~99% specificity). The reflex is preserved in epididymitis. Always check it!
Swelling occurs in both conditions. Not specific.
Fever is more common in infection (epididymitis) than torsion. Absence of fever actually supports torsion.

allDiagnoses.name: Testicular Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: sudden severe pain, high-riding testicle with horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex, adolescent male.

allDiagnoses.name: Testicular Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Salvage rate: <6hrs = 90%, 12-24hrs = 20-50%, >24hrs = <10%. Time is testicle!

allDiagnoses.name: Torsion of Appendix Testis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can mimic torsion but is benign. 'Blue dot sign' at upper pole is pathognomonic. Managed conservatively but if uncertain, explore.

allDiagnoses.name: Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with scrotal pain. Bowel in scrotum is surgical emergency.

allDiagnoses.name: Epididymitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Less common in adolescents than adults. Usually more gradual onset. Cremasteric reflex preserved. Positive Prehn sign (relief with elevation).

allDiagnoses.name: Orchitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Often associated with mumps or epididymitis. More gradual onset.

allDiagnoses.name: Testicular Trauma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have history of trauma. Consider testicular rupture if significant mechanism.

male-16-torsion.oldcartsNarrative: "I woke up at 3am with the worst pain I've ever had in my left testicle. It came on suddenly while I was sleeping. The pain is constant and severe - like a 10 out of 10. I vomited twice from the pain. Nothing makes it better - I tried lying down, standing up, putting ice on it. It hurts to walk. My left testicle looks swollen and it's riding up higher than normal. No burning with urination. I haven't been sexually active. No trauma - I was just sleeping. This has never happened before. I'm otherwise healthy, no medications. I can't even touch it, it hurts so bad."

historyQuestions.text: When and how did the pain start?
historyQuestions.answer: It woke me up at 3am. I was sound asleep and suddenly had the worst pain of my life in my left testicle. It came on all at once - 0 to 10 in seconds.
historyQuestions.reasoning: SUDDEN onset is classic for torsion. Epididymitis is typically more gradual. Nighttime/early morning onset common in torsion.

historyQuestions.text: How severe is the pain?
historyQuestions.answer: 10 out of 10. The worst pain I've ever had. I vomited twice just from the pain. I can't even touch it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severe pain with nausea/vomiting is classic for torsion. The ischemia causes intense visceral pain.

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly is the pain?
historyQuestions.answer: It's in my left testicle. The pain also goes up into my lower belly on the left side.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pain radiating to ipsilateral lower abdomen is common due to referred pain via genitofemoral nerve

historyQuestions.text: Does changing position help?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing helps. I've tried lying down, standing, walking - nothing makes it better. Maybe even worse when I try to walk.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prehn sign (relief with scrotal elevation) is classically NEGATIVE in torsion. Positive in epididymitis.

historyQuestions.text: Any injury to the area?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I was just sleeping. No sports, no hit, nothing. I went to bed fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of trauma supports torsion (spontaneous) vs traumatic testicular injury

historyQuestions.text: Any burning with urination or discharge?
historyQuestions.answer: No burning, no discharge, nothing like that. I haven't had any urinary symptoms.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of urinary symptoms argues against infection (epididymitis/UTI)

historyQuestions.text: Are you sexually active?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've never been sexually active.
historyQuestions.reasoning: In sexually active males, STI-related epididymitis is more common. In adolescents without sexual activity, torsion is more likely than epididymitis.

historyQuestions.text: Has this ever happened before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never. Well, actually, maybe once a few months ago I had pain that went away on its own after about 30 minutes, but nothing like this.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior episode that resolved = possible intermittent torsion! Bell clapper deformity allows torsion/detorsion. This increases risk of complete torsion.

historyQuestions.text: Any injury or trauma to that area?
historyQuestions.answer: No trauma at all. I was just sleeping when it started. Nothing hit me or anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rules out trauma; spontaneous onset classic for torsion

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before surgery and anesthesia

historyQuestions.text: Do you take any medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't take anything. I'm healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication list for anesthesia

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've always been healthy. Never been in the hospital before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: General health for surgical risk assessment

historyQuestions.text: Ever had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries ever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior anesthesia history; no prior orchiopexy

historyQuestions.text: Anyone in your family ever have testicular torsion?
historyQuestions.answer: My older brother had something similar when he was 14. I think he had to have surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bell clapper deformity (predisposing to torsion) can be familial

historyQuestions.text: When did you last eat or drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I had dinner around 7pm last night. Haven't had anything since then except some water when I woke up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NPO status critical for emergent surgery - about 8 hours since last meal

historyQuestions.text: Are your parents here with you?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, my mom drove me here. She's in the waiting room.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Minor patient - need parental consent for emergency surgery

physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and hypertension from pain. AFEBRILE is important - fever would suggest infection (epididymitis).

physicalExam.finding: Adolescent male in obvious distress, lying still, reluctant to move. Appears pale and diaphoretic.
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe pain appearance. Patients with torsion often lie very still as movement worsens pain.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, mild tenderness in left lower quadrant. No rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Referred pain to ipsilateral lower quadrant is common in torsion. No peritoneal signs.

physicalExam.finding: No inguinal hernia palpable. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out incarcerated hernia as cause of scrotal pain

physicalExam.finding: Left hemiscrotum swollen, erythematous. Left testicle HIGH-RIDING and HORIZONTAL LIE (bell clapper position). Exquisitely tender to palpation. Right testicle normal position and non-tender.
physicalExam.reasoning: HIGH-RIDING testicle with HORIZONTAL LIE is pathognomonic for torsion. The spermatic cord shortens as it twists, pulling testicle up.

physicalExam.finding: LEFT cremasteric reflex ABSENT. Right cremasteric reflex intact.
physicalExam.reasoning: ABSENT cremasteric reflex is highly specific (~99%) for torsion. The twisted cord prevents the reflex. This is the key finding!

physicalExam.finding: Elevation of left hemiscrotum does NOT relieve pain (negative Prehn sign).
physicalExam.reasoning: Prehn sign: Relief with elevation = epididymitis (takes tension off epididymis). No relief = torsion. Not 100% reliable but supportive.

physicalExam.finding: No blue dot visible at upper pole of testicle.
physicalExam.reasoning: Blue dot sign = torsion of appendix testis (vestigial structure). Would see blue/black spot at upper pole. Managed conservatively.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Clear, no WBCs, no bacteria, no blood
labs.reasoning: Normal UA argues against epididymitis/UTI. However, do not delay surgery for UA.

labs.name: Labs generally not needed
labs.result: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis. Do not delay surgery for labs.
labs.reasoning: This is a time-sensitive surgical emergency. Labs do not help and should not delay OR.

imaging.name: Scrotal Ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.result: Do NOT obtain if clinical suspicion is high. If obtained: Would show absent or decreased blood flow to left testicle. May see whirlpool sign (twisted cord).
imaging.reasoning: If clinical picture is classic (as in this case), GO TO OR. Ultrasound takes time and false negatives occur in early/partial torsion. Sensitivity ~88-100% but specificity lower.

treatments.name: Manual Detorsion (if surgery delayed)
treatments.result: Attempt 'open the book' maneuver: rotate testicle outward (toward thigh) 180-360 degrees. Relief of pain suggests successful detorsion. Still requires surgery.
treatments.reasoning: Can attempt while awaiting OR. Most torsion is medial rotation, so 'open the book' (lateral rotation) detorses. Even if successful, surgery still needed for orchiopexy.

treatments.name: NPO for surgery
treatments.result: Nothing by mouth in preparation for emergent surgery
treatments.reasoning: Patient needs emergent surgical exploration. NPO status required.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: IV morphine or fentanyl for pain control
treatments.reasoning: Severe pain requires IV opioids. Also helps with exam and manual detorsion attempt.

consults.name: Urology - EMERGENT
consults.result: EMERGENT urology consult for surgical exploration and orchiopexy
consults.reasoning: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Urology must be called immediately. Time is testicle - every minute counts. Salvage rate drops significantly after 6 hours.

disposition.name: Emergent OR
disposition.result: Direct to operating room for scrotal exploration, detorsion, and bilateral orchiopexy
disposition.reasoning: Surgery includes: exploration, detorsion, assessment of viability, orchiopexy (fixation to prevent recurrence), and CONTRALATERAL orchiopexy (bell clapper is often bilateral).

actualDiagnosis.name: Left Testicular Torsion - Surgical Emergency
keyFindings: Sudden onset severe testicular pain (woke from sleep)
Nausea and vomiting from pain severity
No trauma, no urinary symptoms, not sexually active
Prior episode of pain that resolved (intermittent torsion)
Negative Prehn sign (no relief with elevation)
teachingPoints: Testicular torsion is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Time is testicle. Salvage rates: <6hrs = 90-100%, 12-24hrs = 20-50%, >24hrs = <10%.
Bimodal age distribution: neonates (in utero) and adolescents (12-18 years). Most common in teens.
Bell clapper deformity: Tunica vaginalis attaches high on cord, allowing testicle to rotate freely like a bell clapper. Often BILATERAL - that's why contralateral orchiopexy is done.
Classic presentation: Sudden severe pain, nausea/vomiting, high-riding testicle, horizontal lie, absent cremasteric reflex.
Cremasteric reflex: Stroke inner thigh → testicle should retract. ABSENT reflex = highly specific for torsion (~99%). ALWAYS check it!
Prehn sign: Elevation relieves pain in epididymitis (takes tension off), does NOT relieve pain in torsion. Not completely reliable but helpful.
If clinical picture is classic, go DIRECTLY to OR. Do not delay for ultrasound. A negative ultrasound does NOT rule out torsion.
Manual detorsion: 'Open the book' - rotate outward toward thigh. Most torsion is medial, so lateral rotation detorses. Even if successful, still need surgical orchiopexy.
Surgery includes: Exploration, detorsion, viability assessment, orchiopexy (3-point fixation), AND contralateral orchiopexy (bell clapper is usually bilateral).
If testicle is nonviable at surgery: Orchiectomy performed. Can later consider testicular prosthesis.
Torsion of appendix testis: Benign. Blue dot sign at upper pole. Managed conservatively with NSAIDs. If uncertain between this and true torsion, explore surgically.

guidelineReferences.name: AUA Guidelines on Pediatric Acute Scrotum
keyPoints: Testicular torsion is a clinical diagnosis
Absent cremasteric reflex highly specific for torsion
Do not delay surgery for imaging if clinical suspicion high
Bilateral orchiopexy at time of surgery

guidelineReferences.name: AAP Clinical Report: Evaluation of Acute Scrotum
keyPoints: Bimodal distribution: neonates and adolescents
Manual detorsion: rotate outward (open the book)
High-riding horizontal testicle is pathognomonic
Ultrasound should not delay surgical exploration
Bell clapper deformity often bilateral

examFindings.prehn_sign: Positive Prehn sign - pain relieved with testicular elevation.

feedback.correct: Features favor epididymitis: gradual onset, fever, urinary symptoms, positive Prehn. BUT torsion can still occur with infection. If ANY doubt, ultrasound or surgical exploration.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of epididymitis features. What examination finding helps distinguish?
feedback.incorrect: Epididymitis: gradual onset, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn sign, intact cremasteric reflex. Treat with antibiotics (ceftriaxone + doxycycline if STI risk, fluoroquinolone if UTI source). Still consider torsion if doubt exists.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: TORSION vs EPIDIDYMITIS: TORSION = sudden onset (minutes-hours), severe pain, nausea/vomiting, ABSENT cremasteric reflex, high-riding testis, horizontal lie, NO urinary symptoms, often younger (peak 12-18y). EPIDIDYMITIS = gradual onset (days), dysuria/discharge common, cremasteric reflex PRESENT, positive Prehn sign, epididymal tenderness, often sexually active with STI risk.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if symptoms started 36 hours ago?
whatIfScenarios.description: Classic torsion presentation but pain began 36 hours ago

chiefComplaint.original: My left testicle hurts really bad - worst pain ever
chiefComplaint.modified: My testicle has hurt terribly since yesterday morning - about 36 hours now

historyAnswers.onset: The severe pain started yesterday morning - about 18 hours ago.
historyAnswers.why_waited: I thought it would get better. I was embarrassed to come in.
historyAnswers.current_pain: The pain is actually less now than it was yesterday. It's kind of numb.

examFindings.scrotum: Enlarged, edematous, very erythematous left hemiscrotum.
examFindings.testicle: Testicle firm, tender, with surrounding reactive hydrocele.

keyDiscriminators: Physical exam still consistent with torsion

feedback.correct: After 24 hours, salvage rate is <10%. Still explore surgically - some testicles can be saved, and exploration confirms diagnosis. Likely orchiectomy needed.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of poor prognosis. Should surgery still be performed?
feedback.incorrect: Even with >24 hour duration, still explore surgically. Salvage rate is low (<10% after 24h) but not zero. Surgery also: confirms diagnosis, removes nonviable testicle, performs contralateral orchiopexy to prevent future torsion of remaining testicle.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: TIME IS TESTICLE: <6 hours = 90-100% salvage. 6-12 hours = 50% salvage. 12-24 hours = 20% salvage. >24 hours = <10% salvage. PARADOXICALLY IMPROVED PAIN: Pain decreasing after severe onset may indicate infarction. Necrotic tissue = less pain. Does NOT mean patient is better. Still needs surgical exploration - orchiectomy if non-viable, fixation of contralateral testis.

whatIfScenarios.description: Testicular pain with visible blue discoloration at upper pole of testicle

chiefComplaint.original: My left testicle hurts really bad - worst pain ever
chiefComplaint.modified: My testicle hurts - there's a small blue spot I can see at the top

historyAnswers.onset: It started suddenly this morning, about 6 hours ago.
historyAnswers.pain_severity: It hurts, maybe 5-6 out of 10. Bad but not unbearable.
historyAnswers.pain_location: The pain is mostly at the top of my testicle.

examFindings.scrotum: Mild swelling at superior pole of left testicle.
examFindings.tenderness: Focal tenderness at upper pole of testicle. Testicle itself non-tender.
examFindings.blue_dot: Blue dot sign PRESENT - small blue/purple discoloration visible through scrotal skin at superior pole.

feedback.correct: Blue dot sign = torsion of appendix testis (appendix testis or appendix epididymis). BENIGN - manage conservatively with NSAIDs and scrotal support. But if any doubt about true torsion, explore.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of the blue dot sign. What's the management?
feedback.incorrect: Torsion of APPENDIX testis (not the testicle itself) = benign. Blue dot sign is pathognomonic when visible. Cremasteric reflex intact, testicle in normal position. Manage with NSAIDs, ice, scrotal support. If uncertain, explore surgically.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: APPENDIX TESTIS TORSION: Most common cause of acute scrotum in prepubertal boys. Blue dot sign present in only ~21% but pathognomonic when seen. Tenderness localized to upper pole, NOT diffuse. Cremasteric reflex intact. Treatment: CONSERVATIVE (NSAIDs, rest). Self-limited - appendix will infarct and resolve. If ANY doubt about testicular torsion → surgical exploration.

female-72-siadh.name: Daniel Patterson
female-72-siadh.chiefComplaint: My mother is confused and acting strange
female-72-siadh.variantDescription: SIADH from lung cancer causing severe hyponatremia

missedConsequences.Severe Hyponatremia: Can cause cerebral edema, seizures, herniation, and death. Requires careful correction.
missedConsequences.Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome: Correcting sodium TOO FAST causes irreversible pontine and extrapontine myelinolysis. Rate matters!
missedConsequences.Underlying Malignancy: SIADH can be paraneoplastic - often small cell lung cancer. Must find cause.

commonPitfalls: Correcting sodium too fast (>8-10 mEq/L in 24h) → osmotic demyelination syndrome
Not determining the volume status (hypovolemic vs euvolemic vs hypervolemic)
Giving normal saline when the problem is SIADH (can worsen hyponatremia!)
Not checking urine studies (urine Na, urine osmolality)
Missing the underlying cause (lung cancer, drugs, CNS disease)

mcqs.question: A patient has sodium 118, serum osmolality 248 (low), urine osmolality 520 (inappropriately concentrated), urine sodium 65 (high), and is clinically euvolemic. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.explanation: Hypovolemia would have low urine sodium (<20) as kidneys retain sodium. This patient has high urine sodium (65).
Classic SIADH: Euvolemic, low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine (>100), HIGH urine sodium (>40). Kidneys can excrete sodium but not water.
Polydipsia causes hyponatremia but urine should be MAXIMALLY DILUTE (<100 mOsm). This patient has concentrated urine (520).
Heart failure is hypervolemic hyponatremia with edema. This patient is euvolemic.

mcqs.question: Sodium is 118 mEq/L. The patient is symptomatic with confusion. What is the safest approach to correction?

options.explanation: DANGEROUS! Rapid correction causes osmotic demyelination syndrome. Target is slow correction, not normalization.

options.text: Correct by 8-10 mEq/L in first 24 hours maximum
options.explanation: Goal: Correct no more than 8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours (some say 6-8 for high-risk patients). For severe symptoms, can give 3% saline to raise 4-6 mEq/L initially.
Normal saline can WORSEN hyponatremia in SIADH. The kidneys will excrete the sodium but retain the water.
Fluid restriction is treatment for SIADH but too slow for symptomatic patient. Need 3% saline for severe symptoms.

allDiagnoses.name: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Euvolemic, concentrated urine, high urine sodium, low serum osmolality. Classic pattern.

allDiagnoses.name: Severe Hyponatremia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Na 118 with neurologic symptoms (confusion) is severe and dangerous.

allDiagnoses.name: Underlying Malignancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: SIADH is commonly paraneoplastic - small cell lung cancer is classic. Must image chest.

allDiagnoses.name: CNS Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: CNS lesions (tumor, bleed, infection, trauma) can cause SIADH. Consider head imaging.

allDiagnoses.name: Osmotic Demyelination (with overcorrection)
allDiagnoses.rationale: If sodium corrected too fast, irreversible brain damage occurs. Monitor closely and limit correction rate.

allDiagnoses.name: Drug-Induced SIADH
allDiagnoses.rationale: Many drugs cause SIADH (SSRIs, carbamazepine, cyclophosphamide). Review medications.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypothyroidism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause hyponatremia. Check TSH. Usually less severe.

allDiagnoses.name: Adrenal Insufficiency
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cortisol deficiency causes hyponatremia. Check cortisol if suspected.

female-72-siadh.oldcartsNarrative: (From daughter): "My mother has been confused for the past 2-3 days. She's saying things that don't make sense and she's unsteady on her feet - she almost fell yesterday. She's been having headaches too. She vomited once yesterday. She's usually very sharp for her age. She has high blood pressure and takes lisinopril. She was a smoker for 40 years - quit 5 years ago. She's been having a cough for a few months that she said was just from her years of smoking. She's lost some weight but she's been trying to lose weight. No fever. No chest pain. She's been going to the bathroom normally. She's not been drinking a lot of water or anything."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first notice something was wrong?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She started acting confused about 2-3 days ago. It came on gradually - at first we thought she was just tired, but it's gotten worse each day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Subacute onset over days allows assessment of correction rate; rapid onset would be more dangerous

historyQuestions.text: Describe the confusion
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She started acting confused about 2-3 days ago. She doesn't know what day it is, she's repeating herself, she called me by my sister's name. Yesterday she was walking like she was drunk - almost fell twice.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confusion, gait instability = severe hyponatremia symptoms. Cerebral edema from low sodium.

historyQuestions.text: Any headaches?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Yes, she's complained of headaches the past few days. And she vomited once yesterday.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Headache and vomiting are symptoms of cerebral edema from hyponatremia.

historyQuestions.text: Smoking history?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She smoked for 40 years - about a pack a day. She quit 5 years ago, but that's a lot of damage.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy smoking history = high risk for lung cancer. SIADH is paraneoplastic syndrome of small cell lung cancer.

historyQuestions.text: Has she had any respiratory symptoms?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She's had this cough for months. We thought it was just from all those years of smoking. She hasn't seen a doctor about it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic cough in ex-smoker + SIADH = high suspicion for lung cancer. Must image chest.

historyQuestions.text: Any weight changes?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She's lost maybe 10-15 pounds over the past few months. She said she was trying to lose weight but she hasn't really been dieting.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Unintentional weight loss + smoking history + cough + SIADH = cancer red flags

historyQuestions.text: What medications does she take?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Just lisinopril for blood pressure. Maybe some vitamins. She doesn't take any water pills or anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ACE inhibitors don't commonly cause SIADH. SSRIs, carbamazepine, and many others do. Important to review full med list.

historyQuestions.text: How much has she been drinking?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Nothing unusual. Regular amounts. She hasn't been drinking tons of water or anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Normal fluid intake argues against polydipsia as cause.

historyQuestions.text: Any changes in urination?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She hasn't mentioned anything. Seems to be going a normal amount.
historyQuestions.reasoning: In SIADH, urine output may be decreased because ADH causes water retention.

historyQuestions.text: Any seizures or twitching?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) No seizures. But she's had some muscle twitching in her arms.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Seizures are late/severe sign of hyponatremia. Muscle twitching can precede seizures.

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Penicillin gives her a rash. That's the only one.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before any treatment

historyQuestions.text: What other medical problems does she have?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) High blood pressure for years. She had a small stroke about 3 years ago but recovered well. No diabetes.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior stroke is relevant - need to distinguish metabolic encephalopathy from new stroke

historyQuestions.text: Any prior surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Hysterectomy years ago. Appendix out as a child. Nothing recent.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No recent surgeries that would cause SIADH

historyQuestions.text: What was she like before this started?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Sharp as a tack. She does crossword puzzles every day, reads constantly, still drives. This is not like her at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Establishing baseline mental status - acute change from baseline is significant

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of cancer?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) Her brother died of lung cancer. He was a smoker too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history of lung cancer adds to concern given her smoking and symptoms

historyQuestions.text: Who does she live with? How is she functioning at home?
historyQuestions.answer: (Daughter) She lives alone but I check on her every day. Until this week she was completely independent.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important for discharge planning and assessing functional change

physicalExam.finding: BP 128/76, HR 78, RR 16, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 96% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Stable vitals. Not hypotensive (not hypovolemic). SpO2 96% may be slightly low - check lungs.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly female, appears confused but arousable. Thin. No acute distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confusion is the key finding. Thin appearance consistent with weight loss.

physicalExam.finding: Alert but disoriented to date and place. Difficulty following commands. Word-finding difficulty. Asterixis present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Encephalopathy from hyponatremia. Asterixis (flapping tremor) can occur with metabolic encephalopathies.

physicalExam.finding: Mucous membranes moist. JVP normal (~7cm). No peripheral edema. Skin turgor normal. EUVOLEMIC.
physicalExam.reasoning: EUVOLEMIC = key to diagnosis. Hypovolemic would have dry MM, low JVP, poor turgor. Hypervolemic would have edema, elevated JVP.

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds at right base. Dullness to percussion right lower lobe. No wheezing.
physicalExam.reasoning: Decreased breath sounds and dullness = possible mass, effusion, or consolidation. Concerning in smoker with SIADH.

physicalExam.finding: Small, mobile right supraclavicular lymph node palpable (~1.5cm).
physicalExam.reasoning: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (Virchow's node) is concerning for intrathoracic malignancy. Right-sided suggests lung cancer.

physicalExam.finding: No focal motor deficits. Gait unsteady - wide-based. Reflexes symmetric. No meningismus.
physicalExam.reasoning: Gait instability from encephalopathy. No focal signs suggests metabolic rather than structural cause. No meningismus.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 118 (CRITICAL LOW), K 4.2, Cl 84, CO2 24, BUN 8, Cr 0.7, Glucose 102
labs.reasoning: Sodium 118 is severely low (<120 is severe). Low BUN suggests not hypovolemic (not prerenal).

labs.name: Serum Osmolality
labs.result: 248 mOsm/kg (LOW - normal 275-295)
labs.reasoning: Low serum osmolality confirms TRUE hypotonic hyponatremia (not pseudohyponatremia from lipids/proteins).

labs.name: Urine Osmolality
labs.result: 520 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated)
labs.reasoning: Should be <100 if kidneys working properly. >100 means kidneys can't excrete free water = SIADH or other ADH-mediated process.

labs.name: Urine Sodium
labs.result: 65 mEq/L (HIGH - >40)
labs.reasoning: High urine sodium (>40) in hyponatremia = SIADH or adrenal insufficiency. Low urine sodium (<20) would suggest hypovolemia.

labs.name: TSH
labs.result: 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
labs.reasoning: Rules out hypothyroidism as cause of hyponatremia.

labs.name: Morning Cortisol
labs.result: 18 mcg/dL (normal)
labs.reasoning: Rules out adrenal insufficiency. Must check before diagnosing SIADH.

labs.name: Uric Acid
labs.result: 2.1 mg/dL (LOW)
labs.reasoning: Low uric acid supports SIADH (increased renal clearance with volume expansion). High in hypovolemia.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: 3cm right hilar mass. Right lower lobe atelectasis. Small right pleural effusion.
imaging.reasoning: Mass in smoker with SIADH = lung cancer until proven otherwise. Need CT chest.

imaging.name: CT Chest
imaging.result: 3.5cm right hilar mass with mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Consistent with primary lung malignancy. Recommend tissue diagnosis.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms lung mass. Small cell lung cancer is most associated with SIADH (paraneoplastic).

treatments.name: 3% Hypertonic Saline
treatments.result: 3% saline 100mL bolus over 10 min, can repeat x2 for severe symptoms. Goal: raise Na by 4-6 mEq/L initially.
treatments.reasoning: For symptomatic hyponatremia (confusion, seizures), give hypertonic saline. Small boluses are safer than continuous infusion.

treatments.name: Frequent sodium monitoring
treatments.result: Check sodium q2-4 hours during correction. CRITICAL to avoid overcorrection.
treatments.reasoning: Goal: correct no more than 8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours. Overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome.

treatments.name: Fluid restriction
treatments.result: Restrict fluids to 1-1.5L/day for ongoing SIADH management
treatments.reasoning: Fluid restriction is mainstay of SIADH treatment once acute phase managed. The problem is too much water, not too little sodium.

treatments.name: Avoid normal saline
treatments.result: Do NOT give normal saline (0.9% NaCl)
treatments.reasoning: In SIADH, kidneys can excrete sodium but not water. NS can paradoxically WORSEN hyponatremia as water is retained.

treatments.name: Rescue therapy if overcorrected
treatments.result: If sodium rises too fast: D5W infusion or desmopressin (DDAVP) to re-lower sodium
treatments.reasoning: If Na rises >8-10 in 24h, can give DDAVP + D5W to safely re-lower sodium and prevent osmotic demyelination.

consults.name: Nephrology Consult
consults.result: Nephrology consulted for management of severe symptomatic hyponatremia
consults.reasoning: Complex electrolyte management. Help guide correction rate and treatment.

consults.name: Oncology/Pulmonology Consult
consults.result: For tissue diagnosis and staging of lung mass
consults.reasoning: Need biopsy for diagnosis. SIADH may improve with cancer treatment.

disposition.name: ICU admission
disposition.result: Admit to ICU for close monitoring of sodium correction
disposition.reasoning: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia requires ICU-level monitoring with frequent Na checks.

actualDiagnosis.name: SIADH Secondary to Small Cell Lung Cancer with Severe Symptomatic Hyponatremia
keyFindings: Severe hyponatremia: Na 118 with neurologic symptoms (confusion, gait instability, headache)
Euvolemic on exam: normal JVP, no edema, moist mucous membranes
Low serum osmolality (248) = true hypotonic hyponatremia
Inappropriately concentrated urine (520 mOsm) when serum is dilute
High urine sodium (65) = kidneys can excrete sodium, problem is water retention
Normal TSH and cortisol = rules out hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency
Lung mass on imaging in patient with 40 pack-year smoking history
teachingPoints: Hyponatremia approach: 1) Confirm true hyponatremia (check serum osm), 2) Assess volume status, 3) Check urine osm and urine Na
Volume status is KEY: Hypovolemic (dry, low UNa), Euvolemic (SIADH, hypothyroid, adrenal), Hypervolemic (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic)
SIADH criteria: Euvolemic, low serum osm, urine osm >100 (inappropriately concentrated), urine Na >40, normal thyroid/adrenal function
SIADH causes: Malignancy (small cell lung cancer!), CNS disease, pulmonary disease, drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine), pain, nausea, surgery
Symptoms of hyponatremia: Mild (nausea, headache) → Moderate (confusion, lethargy) → Severe (seizures, coma, herniation)
CORRECTION RATE IS CRITICAL: Max 8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours. Overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS).
ODS (formerly central pontine myelinolysis): Irreversible demyelination. Presents days later with dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome.
For severe symptoms: 3% saline 100mL boluses (not continuous infusion). Goal is symptom relief (raise by 4-6 mEq/L), not normalization.
NEVER give normal saline for SIADH - can worsen hyponatremia! Kidneys excrete sodium but retain water.
If overcorrection occurs: Give D5W and/or DDAVP to re-lower sodium and prevent ODS.
Chronic SIADH treatment: Fluid restriction, salt tablets, demeclocycline, or vaptans (tolvaptan).

guidelineReferences.name: European Clinical Practice Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyponatraemia 2014
guidelineReferences.citation: Spasovski G et al. Eur J Endocrinol. 2014;170(3):G1-47
keyPoints: Correction limit: Max 10 mEq/L in first 24h, 8 mEq/L if high ODS risk
Severe symptoms: 3% saline 150mL bolus over 20 min, repeat x2 PRN
SIADH diagnosis requires euvolemia, low serum osm, urine osm >100
First-line chronic treatment: Fluid restriction to <1L/day
Vaptans reserved for SIADH refractory to fluid restriction

guidelineReferences.name: Expert Panel Recommendations for Diagnosis and Management of Hyponatremia 2017
guidelineReferences.citation: Verbalis JG et al. Am J Med. 2013;126(10 Suppl 1):S1-42
keyPoints: Check serum osmolality first to rule out pseudohyponatremia
Volume status assessment determines treatment approach
Avoid normal saline in SIADH - can worsen hyponatremia
ODS risk factors: chronic hyponatremia, alcoholism, malnutrition, hypokalemia
If overcorrected: D5W ± DDAVP to re-lower sodium

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was having seizures?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same hyponatremia but patient is actively seizing

chiefComplaint.original: I've been confused and my legs aren't working right
chiefComplaint.modified: [Patient is actively seizing, sodium returns at 112]

feedback.correct: Hyponatremic seizures = GIVE 3% SALINE NOW. 100-150mL bolus over 10 minutes, can repeat 2-3 times. Goal: raise Na by 4-6 to stop seizures. This is one situation where rapid correction is acceptable.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of emergency. What's the immediate treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Seizures from hyponatremia = emergent 3% hypertonic saline boluses. Give 100-150mL over 10 minutes, repeat as needed. The risk of NOT treating outweighs risk of overcorrection. Seizures will stop when Na rises 4-6 mEq/L.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Asymptomatic/mild symptoms = slow correction, max 8-10/24h. Seizures/herniation = rapid partial correction acceptable (4-6 raise) to stop life-threatening symptoms, then slow down.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if sodium rose 14 mEq/L in 24 hours?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient's sodium was 118, now 132 after 24 hours of treatment

chiefComplaint.original: I've been confused and my legs aren't working right
chiefComplaint.modified: Patient doing better but sodium rose from 118 to 132 in 24 hours (14 mEq/L rise)

feedback.correct: OVERCORRECTION = ODS RISK. Rescue therapy: Give DDAVP (2-4 mcg IV) to prevent further water excretion + D5W infusion to re-lower sodium back toward safe correction rate.
feedback.partial: Good concern for overcorrection. What's the rescue therapy?
feedback.incorrect: Correction >10-12 mEq/L in 24 hours risks OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME (ODS). Can be prevented by re-lowering sodium with DDAVP (stops aquaresis) + D5W (free water). Goal is to slow correction, not reverse gains.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Safe correction = 8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours. Overcorrection = give DDAVP + D5W to re-lower. ODS prevention is possible even after overcorrection if caught early.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was dehydrated?
whatIfScenarios.description: Low sodium with signs of volume depletion (orthostasis, dry mucous membranes)

chiefComplaint.original: I've been confused and my legs aren't working right
chiefComplaint.modified: I've been having diarrhea for 5 days and feel dizzy when I stand

feedback.correct: Hypovolemic hyponatremia = GIVE NORMAL SALINE (opposite of SIADH!). Volume repletion suppresses ADH and allows water excretion. Check urine sodium - should be <20 in hypovolemia.
feedback.partial: Good volume assessment. How does treatment differ from SIADH?
feedback.incorrect: Hypovolemic hyponatremia (GI losses, diuretics, poor intake) = treat with NORMAL SALINE. Volume expansion suppresses ADH, kidneys can excrete water. Urine sodium is LOW (<20) because kidneys are retaining sodium. Opposite of SIADH treatment!

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: SIADH (euvolemic) = fluid restriction, NO normal saline (worsens it). Hypovolemic = normal saline IS the treatment. Volume status determines treatment.

Alpha-blockers (medical expulsive therapy) for distal ureteral stones <10mm
NSAIDs first-line for acute renal colic
Intervention for stones >10mm, persistent obstruction, or infection

Hydration to maintain urine output >2.5L/day for prevention

trans-female-34-stone.name: Kevin Mitchell
trans-female-34-stone.chiefComplaint: Severe pain in my right side that came on suddenly
trans-female-34-stone.transgenderCareNote: Patient is a transgender woman (male anatomy, identifies as female). Use preferred name 'Katie' and she/her pronouns. Clinical considerations: Has male anatomy (testicular exam appropriate, no urine pregnancy test needed). May be on hormone therapy (estrogen) - check for VTE risk factors.

missedConsequences.Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stone): Untreated obstructing stone can lead to hydronephrosis, renal damage, and urosepsis.
missedConsequences.Pyelonephritis: Missed infected stone leads to urosepsis and potentially life-threatening illness.
missedConsequences.Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm: Missed AAA in patient with 'kidney stone' presentation leads to rupture and death.

commonPitfalls: Assuming all flank pain is kidney stones without considering AAA in appropriate patients
Missing fever which suggests infected stone requiring urgent intervention
Not checking for hydronephrosis which may require urgent urology consultation
Forgetting to strain urine to capture stone for analysis
Not counseling on prevention - hydration and dietary modifications

mcqs.question: The patient describes the pain as coming in waves and radiating to his groin. What does this pattern suggest?

options.explanation: Appendicitis pain is typically constant, not colicky.
Colicky pain radiating from flank to groin follows the path of the ureter as peristalsis attempts to move the stone.
MSK pain doesn't radiate to groin or come in waves.
Pancreatitis causes epigastric pain radiating to back, not flank to groin.

mcqs.question: CT shows a 4mm stone in the distal right ureter. What is the likelihood of spontaneous passage?

options.explanation: Stones <5mm have high passage rates.
Passage rate is higher for 4mm stones.
Stones <5mm pass spontaneously about 90% of the time. Size is the primary predictor of passage.
Not all stones pass; some require intervention.

allDiagnoses.name: Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stone)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: sudden severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin with hematuria. Note: Patient has male anatomy despite identifying as female.

allDiagnoses.name: Pyelonephritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must consider infection, especially if fever present. Infected obstructing stone is urologic emergency.

allDiagnoses.name: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
allDiagnoses.rationale: Always consider in older patients or those with risk factors. Can mimic renal colic.

allDiagnoses.name: Appendicitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Right-sided pain but typically periumbilical migrating to RLQ, not flank to groin.

allDiagnoses.name: Musculoskeletal Pain
allDiagnoses.rationale: MSK pain is positional, not colicky, and doesn't radiate to groin.

allDiagnoses.name: Testicular Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would present with testicular pain primarily; exam would show abnormal testicle. NOTE: Patient has male anatomy - testicular exam is appropriate.

allDiagnoses.name: Renal Cell Carcinoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually painless hematuria; acute severe pain less typical.

trans-female-34-stone.oldcartsNarrative: "It started about 4 hours ago, completely out of the blue. I was just sitting at my desk when this horrible pain hit me in my right side. It's the worst pain I've ever felt - I can't get comfortable no matter what position I try. The pain comes in waves - it'll ease up a little then come roaring back. It started in my back on the right side but now it's going down toward my groin. I threw up once from the pain. I noticed some blood in my urine this morning. Nothing like this has ever happened to me before."

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain - is it constant or does it come and go?
historyQuestions.answer: It comes in waves. Builds up, peaks, then eases off. Then starts all over again. It's excruciating.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Colicky pain is hallmark of ureteral obstruction - peristaltic waves against fixed obstruction.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned blood in your urine - tell me more.
historyQuestions.answer: My urine looked pink this morning. Not dark red, but definitely not normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hematuria with flank pain strongly suggests stone. Present in ~85% of kidney stones.

historyQuestions.text: Does the pain go anywhere else?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, it shoots down into my groin and even into my testicle on the right side.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic radiation pattern following ureter from flank to groin/testicle.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any fever or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: No fever. Just the pain and nausea.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of fever argues against infected stone. Fever + obstruction = urologic emergency.

historyQuestions.text: Any burning with urination, frequency, or urgency?
historyQuestions.answer: I've felt like I need to pee more often, but when I go it's just a little. No burning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Frequency and urgency can occur when stone is near bladder (distal ureter).

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever had kidney stones before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never. My dad had them though - he said it was terrible.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Family history increases risk. First-time presentation is common in 30s.

historyQuestions.text: How much water do you typically drink?
historyQuestions.answer: Honestly not much. Mostly coffee and energy drinks. Maybe one glass of water a day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic dehydration is major modifiable risk factor for stones.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your diet.
historyQuestions.answer: Lots of protein - I'm trying to build muscle. Protein shakes, chicken, steak. Not many vegetables.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High protein diet increases uric acid and calcium excretion, raising stone risk.

historyQuestions.text: What medications do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing prescription. I take protein shakes, creatine, vitamin C, and calcium supplements.
historyQuestions.reasoning: High-dose vitamin C and calcium supplements are risk factors for stones

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing pain medications and antibiotics if needed

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm healthy. No chronic conditions. Had my appendix out as a kid.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms healthy patient; gout, hyperparathyroidism would increase stone risk

historyQuestions.text: Any history of gout or joint problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had gout. My joints are fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gout history would suggest uric acid stones

historyQuestions.text: Any history of bowel problems - Crohn's, ulcerative colitis, bariatric surgery?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my gut is fine. Never had any stomach surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GI conditions with malabsorption increase oxalate stone risk

historyQuestions.text: When did you last urinate?
historyQuestions.answer: A couple hours ago. It was painful and I saw blood.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms hematuria and that patient is still making urine (not anuric)

historyQuestions.text: What do you do for work?
historyQuestions.answer: Software developer. Sit at my desk all day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Sedentary work with limited hydration breaks is risk factor

diet_hydration.answer: I drink mostly coffee and energy drinks. Water is boring. Maybe one glass a day if that.
diet_hydration.relevance: Chronic dehydration is single most important modifiable risk factor for kidney stones.

diet_protein_intake.answer: High protein diet - trying to bulk up. Protein shakes twice a day, lots of meat.
diet_protein_intake.relevance: High animal protein increases urinary calcium, oxalate, and uric acid - all promote stone formation.

diet_sodium_intake.answer: I eat a lot of processed food. Fast food, frozen meals. Probably pretty salty.
diet_sodium_intake.relevance: High sodium increases urinary calcium excretion, promoting calcium stone formation.

occupation_current_hydration_opportunity.answer: Software developer. I get in the zone and forget to drink anything for hours.
occupation_current_hydration_opportunity.relevance: Occupations with limited hydration breaks increase stone risk due to concentrated urine.

social_supplement_use.answer: Vitamin C megadoses, calcium supplements, creatine for workouts.
social_supplement_use.relevance: High-dose vitamin C metabolizes to oxalate; calcium supplements without meals increase stone risk.

environmental_climate.answer: I live in Texas. It's hot. I probably sweat a lot and don't replace it.
environmental_climate.relevance: Hot climate increases insensible fluid losses; 'stone belt' in southern US related to this.

recreation_exercise_hydration.answer: I work out most days. Heavy lifting. I sweat a lot but don't drink much during.
recreation_exercise_hydration.relevance: Exercise-related dehydration without adequate replacement promotes concentrated urine and stones.

diet_oxalate_intake.answer: I drink a lot of iced tea. Love spinach smoothies too.
diet_oxalate_intake.relevance: Tea and spinach are high in oxalate, contributing to calcium oxalate stones.

physicalExam.finding: BP 148/92 (elevated - pain), HR 98, RR 18, Temp 98.8°F (afebrile), SpO2 99%
physicalExam.reasoning: Elevated BP and HR consistent with severe pain. Afebrile is reassuring - no infection.

physicalExam.finding: Young man in obvious distress, constantly shifting position, unable to get comfortable. Diaphoretic.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLASSIC: Patient with renal colic is RESTLESS - can't find comfortable position. Different from peritonitis where patient lies still.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-distended. Mild RLQ tenderness but no rebound or guarding. No pulsatile mass.
physicalExam.reasoning: Soft abdomen without peritoneal signs. No pulsatile mass rules out AAA clinically.

physicalExam.finding: Marked right CVA tenderness. Left side non-tender.
physicalExam.reasoning: Unilateral CVA tenderness classic for renal pathology - stone or infection.

physicalExam.finding: Both testicles normal position, non-tender, no masses. Normal cremasteric reflex.
physicalExam.reasoning: IMPORTANT: Patient identifies as female but has male anatomy. Testicular exam IS appropriate and necessary to rule out torsion. Approach sensitively with explanation of clinical need.

physicalExam.finding: No inguinal hernias palpable. No bulges with Valsalva.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rules out incarcerated hernia as cause of groin pain.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Color: pink. RBC: >100/hpf (hematuria). WBC: 2-5/hpf (no pyuria). Nitrite: negative. Leukocyte esterase: negative. Calcium oxalate crystals seen.
labs.reasoning: Hematuria present in 85% of acute stone episodes. Absence of infection signs (WBC, nitrite, LE) is reassuring - no infected stone. Crystal morphology suggests stone type.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 140, K 4.2, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 18, Cr 1.0 (normal), Glucose 92. Calcium 10.2 (high-normal).
labs.reasoning: Normal creatinine rules out obstructive AKI. High-normal calcium warrants parathyroid workup if first stone. Baseline for fluid management.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 9.8 (normal), Hgb 15.2, Plt 245. No left shift.
labs.reasoning: Normal WBC without left shift argues against infected stone. WBC elevated + obstructing stone = EMERGENCY.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.result: 32 U/L (normal). No pancreatitis.
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis in patients with flank/abdominal pain.

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test
labs.result: Not applicable - patient has male anatomy (transgender female).
labs.reasoning: ALWAYS check in patients with uterus/ovaries. This patient is a transgender female with male anatomy - pregnancy test not needed. However, if unsure of anatomy, always ask or check.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis without contrast (Stone Protocol)
imaging.result:
FINDINGS:

RIGHT KIDNEY: Normal size (11.2 cm). 4mm hyperdense calculus in the distal right ureter, approximately 1 cm proximal to the ureterovesical junction. Mild proximal hydroureter and mild hydronephrosis. No perinephric stranding or fat infiltration. No parenchymal abnormality.

LEFT KIDNEY: Normal size (10.8 cm). No hydronephrosis, stones, or masses. Normal cortical thickness. No perinephric fluid or fat stranding.

BLADDER: Normal distension. Wall thickness normal. No intraluminal lesions.

LIVER: Normal size and attenuation. No focal lesions. No biliary ductal dilatation.

GALLBLADDER: No cholelithiasis or wall thickening.

SPLEEN: Normal size. No focal lesions.

PANCREAS: Normal size. No focal masses or ductal dilatation.

ADRENAL GLANDS: Normal bilaterally.

AORTA: Normal caliber. No aneurysmal dilatation. No significant atherosclerotic calcification.

APPENDIX: Normal appendix visualized in RLQ. No signs of appendicitis.

BOWEL: No obstruction or focal wall thickening.

LYMPH NODES: No pathologic lymphadenopathy.

BONY STRUCTURES: No acute fracture or destructive lesion.

IMPRESSION:
1. 4mm obstructing right distal ureteral stone with mild upstream hydronephrosis. High likelihood of spontaneous passage given size and location.
2. Remainder of abdominal and pelvic organs unremarkable.
3. No evidence of alternative etiology for patient's symptoms.
imaging.reasoning: GOLD STANDARD for kidney stones. Non-contrast CT sees all stone types. Provides size, location, degree of obstruction. 4mm at UVJ = 90%+ passage rate.

imaging.name: KUB X-ray
imaging.result: Small calcification visible in right pelvis. Limited sensitivity for ureteral stones.
imaging.reasoning: Can see radio-opaque stones but misses radiolucent stones (uric acid). CT preferred if available.

imaging.name: Renal Ultrasound
imaging.result: Right kidney: mild hydronephrosis. No stone visualized. Left kidney normal.
imaging.reasoning: Detects hydronephrosis but often misses ureteral stones. First-line imaging in PREGNANCY.

procedures.name: Urine Straining Instructions
procedures.result: Patient given strainer. Instructed to strain all urine and save any stone fragments for analysis.
procedures.reasoning: Stone analysis is CRITICAL for prevention - different stone types require different dietary and medical interventions.

procedures.name: IV Access
procedures.result: 18-gauge IV placed for fluids and IV medications.
procedures.reasoning: Need IV for fluid resuscitation, IV analgesics, and antiemetics. Many patients dehydrated from vomiting.

treatments.name: IV Normal Saline Bolus
treatments.result: NS 1L bolus given, then 150 mL/hr maintenance. Hydration promotes stone passage.
treatments.reasoning: Aggressive hydration increases urine flow and promotes stone passage. Treats dehydration from vomiting.

treatments.name: Ketorolac 30mg IV (FIRST-LINE)
treatments.result: Ketorolac 30mg IV given. NSAIDs are FIRST-LINE for renal colic - more effective than opioids.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs are FIRST-LINE - reduce ureteral inflammation and spasm. Studies show more effective than opioids for renal colic. Avoid if renal insufficiency.

treatments.name: Hydromorphone 0.5mg IV PRN
treatments.result: Hydromorphone 0.5mg IV for breakthrough pain. May repeat in 2-3 hours.
treatments.reasoning: Opioids for breakthrough pain not controlled by NSAIDs. Renal colic is extremely painful - adequate analgesia essential.

treatments.name: Ondansetron 4mg IV
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV for nausea. May repeat in 4 hours.
treatments.reasoning: Nausea/vomiting common with renal colic. Treating nausea improves oral hydration and comfort.

treatments.name: Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily (Medical Expulsive Therapy)
treatments.result: Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily started. Relaxes ureteral smooth muscle to facilitate passage.
treatments.reasoning: Alpha-blockers are evidence-based for stones 5-10mm (Medical Expulsive Therapy). Greatest benefit for distal ureteral stones. Continue until stone passes.

treatments.name: Ibuprofen 600mg PO q8h PRN (outpatient)
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 600mg q8h PRN for pain at home. Take with food.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs remain first-line for outpatient pain. More effective than opioids for renal colic.

treatments.name: Hydration Counseling
treatments.result: Counseled to drink 2.5-3L fluids daily. Goal 2.5L urine output for stone prevention.
treatments.reasoning: Most important prevention intervention. High urine volume dilutes stone-forming substances.

consults.name: Urology Follow-up
consults.result: Outpatient urology in 2-4 weeks if stone not passed. Sooner if worsening symptoms.
consults.reasoning: Urology for: stone not passed in 4-6 weeks, stone >6mm, persistent symptoms, recurrent stones. May need ureteroscopy or lithotripsy.

consults.name: Urology STAT (if infected stone)
consults.result: NOT indicated - no signs of infection. Would be EMERGENT if fever + obstruction.
consults.reasoning: EMERGENT urology for: obstructing stone + fever = infected stone requiring urgent decompression (stent or nephrostomy). This is urologic EMERGENCY.

disposition.name: Discharge Home
disposition.result: Discharged after pain controlled. Follow-up urology if stone not passed in 2-4 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Small (<5mm) distal stones with controlled pain and no infection = outpatient management. 4mm stone has 90%+ spontaneous passage rate.

disposition.name: Return Precautions
disposition.result: Return for: fever, inability to urinate, uncontrolled pain, persistent vomiting, or if stone not passed in 4-6 weeks.
disposition.reasoning: Patient must know red flags. Fever + obstruction is emergency. Anuria suggests complete obstruction.

actualDiagnosis.name: Nephrolithiasis (Kidney Stone)
keyFindings: Sudden onset severe colicky flank pain
Pain radiating from flank to groin
Patient restless, unable to find comfortable position
CT showing 4mm stone in distal ureter
teachingPoints: Renal colic patients are RESTLESS - can't get comfortable. Peritonitis patients lie STILL. This is a key distinguishing feature.
Fever + obstructing stone = infected stone = urologic EMERGENCY requiring urgent drainage
Size predicts passage: <5mm = ~90% pass; 5-7mm = ~50%; >7mm = usually need intervention
CT without contrast is gold standard - sees all stone types and rules out other pathology
Most important prevention is HYDRATION - goal is 2.5L urine output daily
Stone analysis guides prevention: calcium oxalate (low oxalate diet), uric acid (low purine), etc.

guidelineReferences.name: AUA/Endourology Society Guideline on Stone Disease 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: Assimos D et al. J Urol. 2016;196(4):1153-1160
keyPoints: CT without contrast is gold standard for diagnosis
Medical expulsive therapy (alpha-blockers) for stones 5-10mm
NSAIDs are first-line analgesics for renal colic
Urgent intervention for infected obstructing stone
Stone composition guides metabolic evaluation

guidelineReferences.name: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2023
guidelineReferences.citation: Türk C et al. European Association of Urology
keyPoints: Spontaneous passage rate: <5mm ~90%, 5-7mm ~50%
Observation period 4-6 weeks for uncomplicated stones
Fluid intake to achieve 2.5L urine output daily
24-hour urine metabolic evaluation for recurrent stones
Ureteral stent or nephrostomy for infected obstructing stone

closureScript.diagnosis: You have a kidney stone - a small 4mm stone that's made its way from your kidney down to the lower part of the ureter, which is the tube that connects your kidney to your bladder. That's what's causing this severe pain.
closureScript.plan: The good news is that a stone this size - 4mm - has about a 90% chance of passing on its own. I'm going to give you strong pain medicine and medication to help relax the ureter so the stone can pass more easily. You need to drink lots of fluids - at least 2-3 liters a day. Strain your urine so we can catch the stone and analyze it to prevent future ones.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back immediately if you develop fever, can't keep fluids down, have uncontrollable pain, or can't urinate at all. These could mean the stone is blocking urine flow completely or there's an infection.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is incredibly painful - kidney stones are often compared to childbirth. The pain will come and go as the stone moves. Once it drops into your bladder, the pain will dramatically improve, and then it will pass when you urinate.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had fever?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same obstructing stone but with fever 102°F and pyuria

chiefComplaint.original: Severe pain in my right side going to my groin
chiefComplaint.modified: Severe flank pain and I've had high fevers with shaking chills

feedback.correct: FEVER + OBSTRUCTING STONE = UROLOGIC EMERGENCY. Infected urine behind obstruction = pyonephrosis. Needs emergent decompression (stent or nephrostomy) plus IV antibiotics. Antibiotics alone won't work.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of emergency. What's required beyond antibiotics?
feedback.incorrect: Fever + obstructing stone = INFECTED STONE = EMERGENCY. Antibiotics cannot penetrate an obstructed infected kidney. Needs urgent decompression (ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy) plus broad-spectrum antibiotics. Can rapidly progress to septic shock.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Uninfected stone = outpatient pain management, high passage rate. Infected obstructing stone = urologic emergency, admission, urgent drainage. Fever is the key discriminator.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the stone was 12mm?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same presentation but stone is 12mm at the UPJ

chiefComplaint.original: Severe pain in my right side going to my groin
chiefComplaint.modified: Severe flank pain, CT shows 12mm stone

feedback.correct: Stones >7-10mm rarely pass spontaneously. This patient needs urologic intervention - either ESWL (extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy) or ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy. Urology consult required.
feedback.partial: Good recognition that large stones won't pass. What are the intervention options?
feedback.incorrect: Stone passage rates: <5mm = 90%, 5-7mm = 50%, >7mm = usually need intervention. 12mm stone will NOT pass spontaneously. Options: ESWL (for renal/proximal stones), ureteroscopy with laser (for ureteral stones), or PCNL (for very large renal stones).

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Small stones (<5mm) = expectant management, high passage rate. Large stones (>7-10mm) = urologic intervention required. Size determines management.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had one kidney?
whatIfScenarios.description: Obstructing stone in patient with solitary kidney

chiefComplaint.original: Severe pain in my right side going to my groin
chiefComplaint.modified: Severe flank pain - and I only have one kidney

feedback.correct: Obstructing stone in SOLITARY kidney = more urgent. Complete obstruction means NO renal function. Monitor creatinine closely. Lower threshold for intervention even with smaller stones.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of increased urgency. Why is this more concerning?
feedback.incorrect: With two kidneys, one obstructed kidney is tolerated. With SOLITARY kidney, obstruction = acute renal failure (no backup). Lower threshold for intervention. Monitor creatinine closely. Complete obstruction = anuria = emergent drainage.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Two kidneys = one can compensate for the other. Solitary kidney = obstruction causes immediate renal failure. Same stone is much more urgent in a solitary kidney.

bph.teachingPoint: Acute urinary retention management: 1) Immediate catheterization (Foley or intermittent), 2) Measure post-void residual, 3) Start alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) before catheter removal, 4) Trial of void in 2-3 days. acute_prostatitis.teachingPoint: Acute bacterial prostatitis is a urologic EMERGENCY. Key points: 1) DO NOT vigorously massage prostate - risk of bacteremia, 2) Treat with fluoroquinolone OR TMP-SMX for 4-6 WEEKS (prostatic penetration required), 3) May need hospitalization if toxic/unable to void, 4) Check for abscess with imaging if not improving in 48-72 hours, 5) Urine culture before antibiotics.
acute_prostatitis.clinicalPearl: The DRE finding of an exquisitely tender, boggy, warm prostate is diagnostic. Be GENTLE - vigorous massage can cause bacteremia. This is different from chronic prostatitis which has minimal prostate findings.

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA/ESCMID Guidelines: Acute Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis 2010
guidelineReferences.citation: Gupta K, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103-e120
keyPoints: CYSTITIS FIRST-LINE: Nitrofurantoin 100mg BID x 5 days (A-I); TMP-SMX DS BID x 3 days if resistance <20% (A-I); Fosfomycin 3g single dose (A-I)
AVOID fluoroquinolones for uncomplicated cystitis - reserve for pyelonephritis/complicated UTI due to collateral damage (A-III)
Beta-lactams (amoxicillin-clavulanate, cephalosporins) are ALTERNATIVES only - inferior efficacy vs other agents (B-I)
Amoxicillin/ampicillin should NOT be used for empiric treatment - high resistance rates (A-III)
PYELONEPHRITIS: Ciprofloxacin 500mg BID x 7 days is first-line if FQ resistance <10% (A-I)
If FQ resistance >10%: Give initial dose of ceftriaxone 1g IV or aminoglycoside before oral therapy (B-III)
TMP-SMX x 14 days appropriate for pyelonephritis ONLY if organism known susceptible (A-I)
ALWAYS obtain urine culture for pyelonephritis; culture optional for uncomplicated cystitis
Consider STI (chlamydia/gonorrhea) in sexually active women with dysuria
Complicated UTI risk factors: male sex, pregnancy, diabetes, immunocompromise, urologic abnormality, recent instrumentation

treatments.name: Nitrofurantoin 100mg PO BID x 5 days (FIRST-LINE)
treatments.result: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals (Macrobid) 100mg BID x 5 days prescribed. Take with food to reduce GI upset.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE for uncomplicated cystitis. Excellent efficacy, low resistance rates, spares broader-spectrum antibiotics. Avoid if CrCl <30 or suspected pyelonephritis (poor tissue penetration).

treatments.name: TMP-SMX DS PO BID x 3 days (alternative)
treatments.result: Alternative if local E. coli resistance <20%. Shorter course than nitrofurantoin.
treatments.reasoning: Effective 3-day regimen. Check local resistance patterns - avoid if resistance >20%. Avoid in 1st trimester pregnancy and sulfa allergy.

treatments.name: Fosfomycin 3g PO x 1 dose (single-dose option)
treatments.result: Single dose option for patient convenience. Slightly less effective than multi-day regimens.
treatments.reasoning: Single-dose convenience. Good for adherence concerns. Slightly lower cure rates than 5-day nitrofurantoin. Safe in pregnancy.

treatments.name: Ciprofloxacin 250mg PO BID x 3 days (AVOID for uncomplicated UTI)
treatments.result: NOT recommended for uncomplicated cystitis due to adverse effects and stewardship.
treatments.reasoning: Fluoroquinolones should be AVOIDED for uncomplicated cystitis (FDA warning). Reserve for pyelonephritis, complicated UTI. Risk of tendinopathy, neuropathy, QT prolongation.

treatments.name: Phenazopyridine 200mg PO TID x 2 days PRN dysuria
treatments.result: Urinary analgesic for symptomatic relief. Warn patient urine will turn orange.
treatments.reasoning: Provides symptomatic relief while waiting for antibiotics to work. Limit to 2 days. Warn about orange urine (can stain contacts/clothes). Not a treatment - just symptom relief.

treatments.name: Hydration counseling

treatments.reasoning: Increased fluid intake may help with symptom relief and bacterial clearance. Traditional advice though evidence is limited.

consults.reasoning: Urology referral for: recurrent UTIs (3+/year), structural abnormalities, complicated infections, persistent hematuria after treatment.

disposition.name: Return precautions

disposition.name: UTI prevention counseling

disposition.name: Recurrent UTI management (if applicable)
disposition.result: If recurrent UTIs: consider post-coital prophylaxis, continuous low-dose prophylaxis, or self-start therapy with culture.
disposition.reasoning: Options for women with frequent recurrent UTIs (≥3/year): post-coital single-dose antibiotics, daily low-dose prophylaxis, or patient-initiated therapy with home culture kits.

Key is ruling out pyelonephritis (fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness) and considering STI in sexually active patients. physicalExam.reasoning: Hypertension common comorbidity. Vitals stable.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. Suprapubic area non-distended. No palpable bladder.
physicalExam.reasoning: Palpable bladder would suggest significant retention.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Negative leukocytes, negative nitrites, no blood, no protein. No evidence of infection or hematuria.
labs.reasoning: Rules out UTI (can cause similar symptoms) and hematuria (would require cystoscopy workup).

labs.name: PSA (with informed consent discussion)
labs.result: 3.2 ng/mL (normal for age <4 ng/mL). Discussed risks/benefits of PSA testing before ordering.
labs.reasoning: PSA screening for prostate cancer. MUST discuss implications before ordering - false positives lead to unnecessary biopsies. Normal here but family history warrants monitoring.

labs.name: Creatinine
labs.result: 1.1 mg/dL (normal). No evidence of obstructive nephropathy.
labs.reasoning: Assess for obstructive uropathy with renal insufficiency. Elevated creatinine would indicate upper tract obstruction.

labs.name: Fasting glucose or HbA1c
labs.result: Fasting glucose 108 (prediabetes range).
labs.reasoning: Diabetes can cause polyuria and nocturia. Also important comorbidity to identify.

imaging.name: Post-void Residual (bladder scan)
imaging.reasoning: Elevated PVR confirms incomplete emptying. Guides treatment decisions. Very high PVR (>300mL) may warrant catheter or urgent urology referral.

imaging.name: Renal Ultrasound
imaging.result: Normal kidneys bilaterally, no hydronephrosis. Mildly trabeculated bladder consistent with chronic obstruction. Prostate estimated 42g.
imaging.reasoning: Assess for upper tract obstruction (hydronephrosis) if PVR very high or renal function abnormal. Trabeculated bladder indicates chronic outlet obstruction.

procedures.name: Uroflowmetry
procedures.result: Peak flow 8 mL/sec (normal >15 mL/sec), voided volume 180mL. Pattern consistent with obstruction.
procedures.reasoning: Objective measure of obstruction severity. Low peak flow (<10 mL/sec) supports significant BPH. Useful for tracking response to treatment.

procedures.name: IPSS/AUA Symptom Score
procedures.result: IPSS score: 18 (moderate symptoms - mild 0-7, moderate 8-19, severe 20-35).
procedures.reasoning: Standardized symptom assessment. Quantifies severity and tracks response to treatment. Score 18 = moderate, warrants treatment.

procedures.name: Cystoscopy (if indicated)
procedures.result: Not indicated currently. Would perform if: hematuria, abnormal PSA, suspected stricture, or before surgical intervention.
procedures.reasoning: Cystoscopy for: hematuria workup, suspected bladder pathology, surgical planning. Not needed for straightforward BPH.

treatments.name: Alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) - FIRST LINE
treatments.result: Tamsulosin 0.4mg daily at bedtime started. Works within days to weeks.
treatments.reasoning: First-line medical therapy. Relaxes prostatic smooth muscle. Works fast (days to 2 weeks). Side effects: orthostatic hypotension (take at bedtime), retrograde ejaculation, floppy iris syndrome (inform ophthalmologist before cataract surgery).

treatments.name: 5-alpha reductase inhibitor (finasteride/dutasteride)
treatments.result: Consider adding finasteride 5mg daily if large prostate or alpha-blocker alone insufficient. Takes 6 months to work.
treatments.reasoning: 5-ARIs actually shrink the prostate (30% reduction). Best for large prostates (>40g). Takes 6 months for effect. Side effects: decreased libido, ED, gynecomastia. Reduces PSA by 50% (adjust for screening).

treatments.name: Combination therapy (alpha-blocker + 5-ARI)
treatments.result: Consider combination for large prostate with significant symptoms. More effective than either alone.
treatments.reasoning: Combination therapy superior to monotherapy for symptom improvement and reducing progression. MTOPS trial showed benefit.

treatments.name: Medication counseling - avoid anticholinergics
treatments.result: Counseled to avoid: diphenhydramine (Benadryl), decongestants (pseudoephedrine), anticholinergics. These can precipitate urinary retention.
treatments.reasoning: Critical patient education. Anticholinergics and decongestants worsen BPH symptoms and can cause acute retention.

treatments.name: Tadalafil 5mg daily (if ED present)
treatments.result: Not started currently. Can consider if erectile dysfunction also present - treats both BPH and ED.
treatments.reasoning: PDE5 inhibitors improve LUTS and treat ED. Good option for patients with both conditions.

treatments.name: Behavioral modifications
treatments.result: Counseled: limit evening fluids, reduce caffeine/alcohol, double voiding, timed voiding.
treatments.reasoning: Behavioral changes can improve symptoms. Reduce fluid intake 2-3 hours before bed. Caffeine and alcohol are bladder irritants.

consults.name: Urology referral
consults.result: Not needed currently - moderate symptoms, good response expected to medical therapy. Refer if: medication failure, recurrent UTIs, recurrent retention, hematuria, elevated creatinine, or patient prefers surgery.
consults.reasoning: Urology referral for: refractory symptoms, surgical candidates, high PVR (>300mL), recurrent retention/UTIs, concerning PSA, or hematuria.

disposition.name: Outpatient management
disposition.result: Outpatient management with alpha-blocker. Follow-up in 4-6 weeks to assess response.
disposition.reasoning: BPH is managed outpatient. Alpha-blockers work within 2 weeks - follow-up to assess response and side effects.

disposition.name: Follow-up plan
disposition.result: Follow-up in 4-6 weeks. Reassess symptoms with IPSS, check PVR, assess medication tolerance. If inadequate response, consider adding 5-ARI or urology referral.
disposition.reasoning: Track response to treatment. IPSS should improve. If not better or side effects intolerable, adjust therapy.

disposition.name: PSA monitoring
disposition.result: Annual PSA given family history. Discuss shared decision-making. If starting 5-ARI, baseline PSA needed (5-ARI reduces PSA 50%).
disposition.reasoning: Family history increases prostate cancer risk. Annual PSA reasonable. If on 5-ARI, double the PSA value to estimate true PSA.

disposition.name: Red flag symptoms to return for
disposition.result: Return for: inability to urinate (acute retention), blood in urine, fever with urinary symptoms, significant worsening of symptoms.
disposition.reasoning: Acute retention is emergency. Hematuria requires cystoscopy. Fever suggests complicated UTI.

disposition.name: Surgical options discussed
disposition.result: Discussed TURP and other surgical options if medications fail. Most patients do well with medical therapy.
disposition.reasoning: Patient education about surgical options. TURP is gold standard. Newer options: laser procedures, UroLift, Rezum. Reserve for medication failure or patient preference.

male-68-frequency.teachingPoint: On your urology and primary care rotations, you'll see BPH daily. Key points: 1) DRE is mandatory - you're looking for cancer, 2) PSA requires informed consent discussion, 3) PVR guides management (>200mL concerning), 4) Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) work fast but watch for orthostatic hypotension, 5) 5-ARIs (finasteride) take 6 months to work but actually shrink the prostate, 6) TURP is the gold standard surgery but medical management works for most.
male-68-frequency.clinicalPearl: The 'can't go, gotta go' of BPH: CAN'T GO (obstructive) - hesitancy, weak stream, straining, dribbling, incomplete emptying. GOTTA GO (irritative/storage) - frequency, urgency, nocturia. Both patterns occur because the bladder becomes overactive trying to push urine past the obstruction.

mcqs.question: A 72-year-old man presents unable to void for 12 hours with severe suprapubic pain. Bladder scan shows 900mL. What is the MOST important initial step?

options.text: Order CT scan to evaluate for obstruction
options.explanation: Imaging should not delay catheterization in symptomatic retention.
Relieve the obstruction first! Delayed decompression risks bladder damage and renal injury. Catheterize immediately, then work up the cause.

options.text: Start IV antibiotics for presumed UTI
options.explanation: Retention itself doesn't indicate infection. Catheterize first, then check urine.
Emergent cystoscopy is rarely needed. Foley catheter placement is first step.

mcqs.question: After catheter placement with 1,200mL initial output, urine output is 400mL/hour. What is this phenomenon and management?

options.explanation: This rate of diuresis can lead to significant volume and electrolyte depletion.

options.text: Post-obstructive diuresis - monitor closely, replace fluids and electrolytes
options.explanation: Post-obstructive diuresis occurs in ~50% of patients with severe retention. Can lose liters of fluid. Monitor I/Os, check electrolytes q6h, replace with IV fluids (usually 0.45% NS at 50-75% of urine output).
This is physiologic response to obstruction relief, not from IV fluids.

options.text: Sign of renal failure - urgent nephrology consult
options.explanation: This is appropriate recovery response, not renal failure. Failure would show oliguria.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Urinary Retention Secondary to BPH, Precipitated by Decongestant
keyFindings: 72-year-old with chronic LUTS (nocturia, weak stream) = underlying BPH
Palpable bladder to umbilicus, 950mL on bladder scan
Enlarged prostate on DRE without nodules
Mild AKI (Cr 1.6 from 1.1) with bilateral hydronephrosis
Normal neurologic exam excludes neurogenic cause
teachingPoints: ACUTE URINARY RETENTION IS A UROLOGIC EMERGENCY - catheterize immediately, don't wait for workup
MEDICATION CAUSES: Alpha-agonists (decongestants), anticholinergics (antihistamines, sleep aids, bladder meds), opioids
POST-OBSTRUCTIVE DIURESIS: Occurs in ~50% with severe retention. Can lose liters of fluid. Monitor I/Os hourly, check lytes q6h
POD MANAGEMENT: Replace 50-75% of urine output with 0.45% NS. Watch for hypokalemia, hyponatremia
ALPHA-BLOCKER BEFORE TWOC: Start tamsulosin 48-72h before trial without catheter - increases success from 40% to 60%
AKI FROM OBSTRUCTION: Bilateral hydronephrosis causes post-renal AKI. Creatinine should improve after decompression
TWOC (TRIAL WITHOUT CATHETER): Attempt at 2-3 days after starting alpha-blocker. If fails, may need TURP
AVOID OFFENDING MEDS: Counsel patients with BPH to avoid decongestants and first-gen antihistamines
